vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml

Project 1_3D_Mylonets
Goals:
Describe myeloid cell subtypes and their functional heterogeneity in microenvironment of ovarian cancer 
Create a workflow (wet lab and data analysis) for 3D reconstruction of tumor microenvironment
Main goal of 3-month rotation project: perform 3D visualization of TME, especially myelonets (myeloid cell aggregates)
Responsible:
Project manager
 : Anastasiia Kachalova macro:mentionmacro:velocitymacro:html@anaskach@helsinki_fi
Role/tasks: 
tCycIF experiments, computational data analysis, sample selection for a preliminary 3-month project
Ada Junquera
Role/tasks: 
main advisor - help with protocols/data analysis
Estimated timeline:
Duration of the initial project - 3 months (September 2023 - November 2023, incl.)
macro:task
#2376
Design the panel - till early October
macro:task
#2377
Validation of antibodies: CD14-unconjugated (Abcam, ab133335, clone EPR3653), Lysozyme_C-647 (Santacruz, sc-518012 AF647, clone E-5), Vimentin-750 (CST, 69227S, clone D21H3): 26th Sept - 29th Sept
macro:task
#2378
Validation of CD15 (Biolegend, 301910, clone H198) and new ANXA2 (Santacruz, sc-47696 AF488, clone 3D5): 13th-16th October
macro:task
#2379
Experiment: 16th Oct - 13th November
macro:task
#2380
Zoom meeting with Clarence (Boston) about 3D reconstruction approaches: November 14th
macro:task
#2381
3D reconstruction with simple software (3D Slicer)
macro:task
#2382
Registration of slides in Z axis with custom MATLAB script
macro:task
#2383
3D visualisation with Imaris
macro:task
#2384
Data analysis (cell segmentation, cell type calling, quantification)
---------------------------------------------
Slides used in experiments:
S107_iOme: 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64, 72, 80, 88, 96, 104, 112, 120

ation with Imaris

### word/document.xml
Meeting with Anniina 20.09.23
Main aim
: 
Myeloid cell subtypes identification and their functional heterogeneity in 3D
1 or 2 samples for initial 3D analysis (we selected S026 and S107)
Stainings will be started after Inga-Maria will help me with understanding the distance which is required for myeloid phenotype changes in 2D (we will apply the same distance for z-scale for 3D)
Exclude markers for lymphocytes (FOXP3, CD4, CD8, CD45RO, PCNA) and add functional markers for myeloid cells (pSTAT1, ANXA2, TIM3)
Functional markers should go first in cycles and discrete markers (+/-) - later
Prepare questions for 3D CyCiF before 3rd October!
Meeting with Ada and Inga-Maria 25.09.23
We will profile 2 samples (
S026
: gBRCA1, no progression and 
S107
: BRCAwt, progression PFS 10.49)
We will profile 600 
μm of each block
Slides will be 5 μm in thickness
Thus, we will get 120 slides from one block
For 3D we will use each 8th slide - for one block we will use for analysis 15 slides which is OK
Thus, the distance between slides for 3D will be 35 μm
So we will ask to cut the block continuously, but if it is technically impossible for any reason our 
plan B
 is to ask to do 3 slides continuously and then skip 20 μm and repeat.
Today I will discuss with Saun blocks (she will send them this week to be cut) and I will order Annexin A2 antibody, cause we do not have it in the lab.
The panel is almost finalised, I just need to discuss the order with Ada.
Some antibodies, in which we are not sure, will be tested together with Adhiti's validation this week.

anastasiia.kachalova@helsinki.fi

2. Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helisnki, Finland Suppressor Cells: Key Drivers of Immunosuppression in Ovarian Cancer. Front. Immunol. 10:1273. doi: 10.3389/fimmu.2019.01273

3. Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland 2. Barros MHM, Hauck F, Dreyer JH, Kempkes B, Niedobitek G (2013) Macrophage Polarisation: an Immunohistochemical Approach for
 Identifying M1 and M2 Macrophages. PLoS ONE 8(11): e80908. doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0080908
4. Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland
 3. Lin JR, Wang S, Coy S, Chen YA, Yapp C, Tyler M, Nariya MK, Heiser CN, Lau KS, Santagata S, Sorger PK. Multiplexed 3D atlas of state
5. iCAN digital precision cancer medicine, Helsinki, Finland transitions and immune interaction in colorectal cancer. Cell. 2023 Jan 19;186(2):363-381.e19. doi: 10.1016/j.cell.2022.12.028

## Slide 1
Myelonets
3D CyCiF

## Slide 2
The most important result from 3D!
We conclude that
EMT-like transitions and TB in CRC1 is characterized not by the formation of isolated spheres of cells
, as first described by Weinberg and colleagues in tissue culture,
48
but instead by the formation of large fibrillar structures that appear to be small buds when viewed in cross-section
at their distal tips.
Fibrils can invade into several different environments, including stroma and mucin, and we speculate that their formation is driven by a gradual (not abrupt) breakdown in cell adhesion associated with a graded EMT-like transition

## Slide 3
3D on immune composition
However, we found that many of these domains were interconnected, forming larger 3D structures; for example, seven large networks, each spanning >12 sections and several millimeters laterally.
TLSs undergo maturation and are expected to differ from one another, but when we mapped marker expression clusters onto the physical organization of TLSNs, we found that some were relatively homogeneous, containing cells from one expression cluster, whereas others were heterogeneous.
For example, TLSN-C, which was predominantly located in the subserosa, was >96% composed of expression cluster 7 and showed a marked predominance of CD45+ CD20+ B cells

## Slide 4
Immune profiling of the invasive margin
(L) High-resolution 3D imaging of PDL1:PD1 interaction among tumor and myeloid cells.
Top: maximum intensity projections. Bottom: 3D rendering, Imaris

## Slide 5
Cell types involved in presenting PDL1 to PD1+ T cells
Although a small minority (1%–5%) of tumor cells expressed PDL1,
the cells most likely to be PDL1+ were CD68+ (14%–51% positive) and CD11c+ myeloid cells (10%–88% positive);

, Imaris

## Slide 5
Cell types involved in presenting PDL1 to PD1+ T cells
Although a small minority (1%–5%) of tumor cells expressed PDL1,
the cells most likely to be PDL1+ were CD68+ (14%–51% positive) and CD11c+ myeloid cells (10%–88% positive);
PDL1+ myeloid cells were also 6.5-fold more abundant on average than PDL1+ tumor cells (Figures 7F and S6E).
Second t
he most important result from the article!
Across the CRC cohort, we found that single positive CD68+ CD11c or CD68CD11c+ and double positive CD68+ CD11c+ cells were commonly PDL1+

## Slide 6
PD1-PDL1 binding
Functionally, it is not the prevalence of PDL1+ cells that is relevant for T cell suppression but rather which cells are close enough for PDL1:PD1 binding
cells interacting with PD1+ cells were strongly enriched for CD45+ and depleted for CK+ (p < 0.001 pairwise t test, two-sided),
showing that PD1+ T cells interact with PDL1+ immune cells more commonly than PDL1+ tumor cells
.
Finally, high-resolution optical sectioning of 12-plex CyCIF provided direct evidence of PDL1+ on myeloid cells colocalizing with PD1+ T cells at the tumor margin, consistent with the formation of functional cell-cell interactions (Figure 7L).
We conclude that immunosuppression of PD1+ T cells in our CRC cohort most commonly involves PDL1+ myeloid cells, not tumor cells

## Slide 7
Main insights from the article
EM transition (EMT) occurs on the tumor margin (cells are still connected, not isolated, but form fibrils - result from 3D reconstruction,
not visible in 2D
)
Question that can be asked here - can we found also any spatially resolved structures of immune cells that will show association between immune cell type and cells undergoing EMT?

but form fibrils - result from 3D reconstruction,
not visible in 2D
)
Question that can be asked here - can we found also any spatially resolved structures of immune cells that will show association between immune cell type and cells undergoing EMT?
Immunosuppression in tumors is provided by interaction of PD1+ T cells with PDL1+ myeloid cells, all myeloid cells expressed PDL1.
Question -
what type of myeloid cells
do we see in myelonets (suppressive or not)? Can we characterise them in more details? Do they have something in common with EMT regions?

## Slide 8
More detailed small questions
Myelonets - in which group of patients they are presented?
responders vs. non-responders
/ before therapy vs. after therapy (
paired samples are required
) / mutated vs. wt)
Abundance of myeloid cells between groups
Which myeloid cell types they are consists of?
Include M1(
CD68+, anti-tumor*
) and M2 (CD163+, pro-tumor) markers
MDSC
cells
-?
Cell-cell interactions (?)
Myeloid cells - tumor cells
Myeloid cells -
Ki67
+ tumor cells
Myeloid cells - lymphocytes populations
Myeloid cells - PD1+ T cells
Myeloid PDL1+ cells - PD1+ T cells
3D reconstruction - do we see any specific region of tumor which interacts with myeloid cells? May be EMT regions?

## Slide 9
*
Better distinguish between M1 and M2
Transcription factors should also be studied
pSTAT1 for M1
CMAF for M2
https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0080908
(IHC)

## Slide 10
Existing studies
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-022-05680-3
the study was done on brain tumors, but a lot of
attention
was paid to the macrophages in TME and their association with prognosis.
Kaplan–Meier analysis based on
MPO+ IBA1+ cell frequency

g studies
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-022-05680-3
the study was done on brain tumors, but a lot of
attention
was paid to the macrophages in TME and their association with prognosis.
Kaplan–Meier analysis based on
MPO+ IBA1+ cell frequency
as determined by IHF staining in 135 tumours from patients with glioblastoma (z-score). Cell frequencies were averaged when multiple samples corresponded to the same individual. Log-rank (Mantel–Cox) test
Studied also cell interactions and showed that they are not random and do not correlate with cell frequencies

samples for pre and post (??)
Showed that CD68+ macrophages “lakes” are associated with good prognosis following chemotherapy
Frequency of CD163+ (M2) TAMs decreased after chemotherapy (but samples were unmatched!)

## Slide 15
3D: distance between sections
"Due to whole slide structural changes that biologically occur in the
5 μm space between sections
, it was not possible to adequately register whole slide images this accurately without using non-rigid transformations, which resulted in imaging artifacts that skewed analysis."
https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.12.09.519807v1.abstract
Average cell size of lymphoid cells is 8-10
μm
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459471/#:~:text=From%20the%20morphological%20point%20of,mitochondria%2C%20ribosomes%2C%20and%20lyzosomes
.

## Slide 16
3D visualization
“For CRC1, 106 serial sections were cut from an 1.7 x 1.7 cm piece of FFPE tissue and 22 H&E and
25 CyCIF images
were collected,
skipping some sections to increase the total dimension along the Z-axis
”
Imaris:
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1cyKqe58luBxH4ikf0TSJvxh6Z2zW6Pd-dG1_I92nTWo/edit?usp=sharing
https://www.cell.com/cell/pdf/S0092-8674(22)01571-9.pdf

## Slide 17
Why 3D ?
"At the tumor invasive margin, where tumor, normal, and immune cells compete, T cell suppression involves multiple cell types and

Jvxh6Z2zW6Pd-dG1_I92nTWo/edit?usp=sharing
https://www.cell.com/cell/pdf/S0092-8674(22)01571-9.pdf

## Slide 17
Why 3D ?
"At the tumor invasive margin, where tumor, normal, and immune cells compete, T cell suppression involves multiple cell types and
3D imaging shows that seemingly localized 2D features such as tertiary lymphoid structures are commonly interconnected and have graded molecular properties
"
Our data show that 3D reconstruction provides additional insight into the large-scale connectivity of biological structures, but for relatively straightforward tasks such as cell-type enumeration, 2D WSI is often adequate
